Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}{{ill|lt=Nishinoshima|Q543193|WD=Q543193|1=Nishinoshima, Shimane}}409 Beppu |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Unknown |社格 = {{ill|lt=small Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} / former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|WD=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}} copper-plate roofing |別名 = |例祭 = July 21 |神事 = Shinkōsai (boat procession festival) (every other year, July 21-22)
Omushi-sai (autumn grand festival) (October 21) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Kai Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Kai Shrine''' (かいじんじゃNishinoshima Tourism Association, "[http://nkk-oki.com/japan/download/ Useful information] Town-walk map, Beppu Port / Mitajiri.") is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Beppu, {{ill|lt=Nishinoshima|Q543193|WD=Q543193|1=Nishinoshima, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It stands east of {{ill|lt=Beppu Port (Shimane)|Q11396732|WD=Q11396732|lt=Beppu Port|1=Beppu Port}} and north of the remains of {{ill|lt=Kuroki Gosho|Q11678318|WD=Q11678318|1=kuroki no gosho}}, and is a former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|lt=village shrine|1=Son-sha}} identified with "Kai Shrine, two seats" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' (a {{ill|lt=candidate Shikinaisha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|lt=Shikinaisha candidate|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}). === Enshrined deity === Unknown. The first literary appearance of this shrine is in the 1703 'Dōzen Muramura Shinmei-ki'A record of shrines in {{ill|lt=Dōzen|Q11476216|WD=Q11476216|1=Dōzen}} and their deities., where it is listed as "Rokusha Daimyōjin of Beppu Village" and identified with the Engishiki Kai Shrine, enshrining "one seat of Shiga three deities and one seat of Sumiyoshi three deities." This assigns the two seats in the Engishiki entry to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Watatsumi Sanjin|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}, for six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q4817|WD=Q4817|1=Column}} total. The 1869 'Dōzen Kyūsha Torishirabechō' also reports six deity statues about nine sun in height. Another report by this shrine's priest in early Meiji says that until Meiji they chanted the deity as "Watatsumi no kami no yashiro futakura," but since formal identification in Meiji 5 as Engishiki "Kai Shrine," it has been treated as "deity unknown." The same 'Kyūsha Torishirabechō' reports two cord mirrors in addition to six statues, so if this truly is the Engishiki shrine, those two mirrors may have been the original two sacred objects of "Kai Shrine, two seats." It has thus been pointed out that the old phrase "Watatsumi no kami no yashiro futakura" may mean "Sukunabikona deities, two seats," suggesting the original deity may have been {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuna-dōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}.'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.' === Origin and history === There is a {{ill|lt=kofun|Q1141225|WD=Q1141225|1=Kofun}} behind the {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, and {{ill|lt=obsidian|Q53754|WD=Q53754|lt=obsidian|1=Obsidian}} {{ill|lt=stone arrowheads|Q11587274|WD=Q11587274|1=stone arrowhead}} have been excavated from adjacent land, suggesting an ancient shrine site. If it is truly a Shikinaisha, it may have been worshiped by indigenous {{ill|lt=ama|Q1577970|WD=Q1577970|1=Ama (diving)}} seafaring people.[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/folder/tyosi.html#西ノ島の神社（松浦康麿） Matsūra Yasumaro, "Shrines of Nishinoshima"] In the {{ill|lt=kuninai jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}} '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onshū Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}},' "Senior Third Upper Kaihara Myōjin" and "Senior Fourth Upper Unkaihiko Myōjin" appear, so it may correspond to one of theseMatsūra Yasumaro identifies it with the former; {{ill|lt=Suzuka Tsurane|Q6536446|WD=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}} identifies it with the latter in '{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}'., but premodern origins and development remain unknown, and there are different views on Shikinaisha identification (see below). In the early modern period, it is confirmed to have been called '''Rokusha Daimyōjin''', and from the above 'Dōzen Muramura Shinmei-ki' onward it was identified as a Shikinaisha. In October Meiji 5 it was renamed "Kai Shrine" and ranked as village shrine; since then it has been revered as the tutelary shrine of Beppu. === Alternative theory for Shikinaisha identification === In textual variants of the Unshū-family edition of the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'The Unshū-family edition was collated and block-printed across the reigns of two Matsue-domain lords, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Naritsune|Q11530461|WD=Q11530461|1=Matsudaira Naritsune}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Matsudaira Naritaka|1=UNKNOWN}}., the Chibu District entry for "{{ill|lt=Yurahime Shrine|Q11577626|WD=Q11577626|1=Yurahime Shrine}}" has the note "formerly named Watasu deity." Because the '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onshū Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}}' for Chibu District lists "Yurahime Daimyōjin" separately from "Watasu Myōjin," this is thought to be a note mistakenly shifted from the next line "Kai Shrine" (in the Engishiki, Kai Shrine is listed adjacent to Yurahime Shrine). Based on this, '{{ill|lt=Dai Nihon Shi|Q1156003|WD=Q1156003|lt=Dai Nihon Shi Jingishi|1=Dai Nihonshi}}' and {{ill|lt=Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747|WD=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}}'s '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dainobu|Q11588823|WD=Q11588823|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|1=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dodai}}' argue that {{ill|lt=Shimazu-jima|Q131133623|WD=Q131133623|1=Category:Shimazu Island}} in {{ill|lt=Chibu Village|Q1204968|WD=Q1204968|1=Chibu, Shimane}}, formerly called "Watari-jima"'{{ill|lt=Onshū Shichō Gōki|Q11659057|WD=Q11659057|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki}}', vol.4, Dōzen notes on Chibu harbor and mountains., has Watari Shrine at its {{ウィキ座標|36|00|02.9|N|133|02|56.1|E|scale:25000|western end}}, and that this is likely "Watasu Myōjin" and thus the Engishiki Kai Shrine. === Rituals === === Religious rites === ;Annual festival (July 21) :The 1869 'Kyūsha Torishirabechō' states a "grand festival" was held every other year from June 20 to 21. This refers to the boat procession festival. After adoption of the {{ill|lt=Gregorian calendar|Q210953|WD=Q210953|1=Solar calendar}}, the annual festival has been held each year on July 21, and in alternating years the boat procession is held across July 21-22. In years with boat procession, {{ill|lt=Oki Dōzen Kagura|Q11659047|WD=Q11659047|1=Oki-Dozen Kagura}} is dedicated on the night of the 21st. The origin of this boat rite is unknown, but records say a practice that had lapsed in the early modern period was revived in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317|1=Kōka}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1847|Q7644|WD=Q7644|1=1847}}). ;Omushi Festival (October 21) :New rice totaling 1 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|WD=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} 2 {{ill|lt=shō|Q995076|WD=Q995076|1=Sheng (volume)}} is cooked, divided into 12 trays, and offered before the deity. It was formerly held as a minor festival on September 29 ('Kyūsha Torishirabechō'), but after the Gregorian calendar it has been conducted on the current date as the autumn grand festival. === Shrine priests === The Uno family served by hereditary succession. Their family surname was Fujiwara. Tradition says they came to Oki in the late 16th century while fleeing {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, and served from the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun era|1=Kanbun (era)}} (late 17th century) onward ('Kyūsha Torishirabechō'), but left office in early Meiji. === Shrine buildings === The honden is a 1-ken-by-2-ken {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|WD=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper-plate roofing|1=UNKNOWN}}, rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Keiō|Q776775|WD=Q776775|1=Keiō}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1867|Q7715|WD=Q7715|1=1867}}). It connects through a {{ill|lt=heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}. Both heiden and haiden are copper-plate roofed. There is also a mikoshi storehouse. === Precinct shrines === Ise Shrine, Inari Shrine === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.21 San'indō 4, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1983 === External links === *[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/folder/tyosi.html#西ノ島の神社（松浦康麿） Shrines of Nishinoshima (Matsūra Yasumaro)] (Yakihi Shrine) (accessed September 12, 2009) === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043|WD=Q11659043|1=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province}} {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kai Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kai Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishinoshima]] [[Category:西ノ島町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:pages]]

'ja'} [[JA:海神社 (西ノ島町)]] {{wikidata link|Q11559097}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Kai Shrine |画像 = [[File:海神社.jpg|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}{{ill|lt=Nishinoshima|Q543193|WD=Q543193|1=Nishinoshima, Shimane}}409 Beppu |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Unknown |社格 = {{ill|lt=small Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} / former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|WD=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}} copper-plate roofing |別名 = |例祭 = July 21 |神事 = Shinkōsai (boat procession festival) (every other year, July 21-22)
Omushi-sai (autumn grand festival) (October 21) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Kai Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Kai Shrine''' (かいじんじゃNishinoshima Tourism Association, "[http://nkk-oki.com/japan/download/ Useful information] Town-walk map, Beppu Port / Mitajiri.") is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Beppu, {{ill|lt=Nishinoshima|Q543193|WD=Q543193|1=Nishinoshima, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It stands east of {{ill|lt=Beppu Port (Shimane)|Q11396732|WD=Q11396732|lt=Beppu Port|1=Beppu Port}} and north of the remains of {{ill|lt=Kuroki Gosho|Q11678318|WD=Q11678318|1=kuroki no gosho}}, and is a former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|lt=village shrine|1=Son-sha}} identified with "Kai Shrine, two seats" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' (a {{ill|lt=candidate Shikinaisha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|lt=Shikinaisha candidate|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}). === Enshrined deity === Unknown. The first literary appearance of this shrine is in the 1703 'Dōzen Muramura Shinmei-ki'A record of shrines in {{ill|lt=Dōzen|Q11476216|WD=Q11476216|1=Dōzen}} and their deities., where it is listed as "Rokusha Daimyōjin of Beppu Village" and identified with the Engishiki Kai Shrine, enshrining "one seat of Shiga three deities and one seat of Sumiyoshi three deities." This assigns the two seats in the Engishiki entry to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Watatsumi Sanjin|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}, for six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q4817|WD=Q4817|1=Column}} total. The 1869 'Dōzen Kyūsha Torishirabechō' also reports six deity statues about nine sun in height. Another report by this shrine's priest in early Meiji says that until Meiji they chanted the deity as "Watatsumi no kami no yashiro futakura," but since formal identification in Meiji 5 as Engishiki "Kai Shrine," it has been treated as "deity unknown." The same 'Kyūsha Torishirabechō' reports two cord mirrors in addition to six statues, so if this truly is the Engishiki shrine, those two mirrors may have been the original two sacred objects of "Kai Shrine, two seats." It has thus been pointed out that the old phrase "Watatsumi no kami no yashiro futakura" may mean "Sukunabikona deities, two seats," suggesting the original deity may have been {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuna-dōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}.'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.' === Origin and history === There is a {{ill|lt=kofun|Q1141225|WD=Q1141225|1=Kofun}} behind the {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, and {{ill|lt=obsidian|Q53754|WD=Q53754|lt=obsidian|1=Obsidian}} {{ill|lt=stone arrowheads|Q11587274|WD=Q11587274|1=stone arrowhead}} have been excavated from adjacent land, suggesting an ancient shrine site. If it is truly a Shikinaisha, it may have been worshiped by indigenous {{ill|lt=ama|Q1577970|WD=Q1577970|1=Ama (diving)}} seafaring people.[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/folder/tyosi.html#西ノ島の神社（松浦康麿） Matsūra Yasumaro, "Shrines of Nishinoshima"] In the {{ill|lt=kuninai jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}} '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onshū Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}},' "Senior Third Upper Kaihara Myōjin" and "Senior Fourth Upper Unkaihiko Myōjin" appear, so it may correspond to one of theseMatsūra Yasumaro identifies it with the former; {{ill|lt=Suzuka Tsurane|Q6536446|WD=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}} identifies it with the latter in '{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}'., but premodern origins and development remain unknown, and there are different views on Shikinaisha identification (see below). In the early modern period, it is confirmed to have been called '''Rokusha Daimyōjin''', and from the above 'Dōzen Muramura Shinmei-ki' onward it was identified as a Shikinaisha. In October Meiji 5 it was renamed "Kai Shrine" and ranked as village shrine; since then it has been revered as the tutelary shrine of Beppu. === Alternative theory for Shikinaisha identification === In textual variants of the Unshū-family edition of the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'The Unshū-family edition was collated and block-printed across the reigns of two Matsue-domain lords, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Naritsune|Q11530461|WD=Q11530461|1=Matsudaira Naritsune}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Matsudaira Naritaka|1=UNKNOWN}}., the Chibu District entry for "{{ill|lt=Yurahime Shrine|Q11577626|WD=Q11577626|1=Yurahime Shrine}}" has the note "formerly named Watasu deity." Because the '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onshū Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}}' for Chibu District lists "Yurahime Daimyōjin" separately from "Watasu Myōjin," this is thought to be a note mistakenly shifted from the next line "Kai Shrine" (in the Engishiki, Kai Shrine is listed adjacent to Yurahime Shrine). Based on this, '{{ill|lt=Dai Nihon Shi|Q1156003|WD=Q1156003|lt=Dai Nihon Shi Jingishi|1=Dai Nihonshi}}' and {{ill|lt=Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747|WD=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}}'s '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dainobu|Q11588823|WD=Q11588823|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|1=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dodai}}' argue that {{ill|lt=Shimazu-jima|Q131133623|WD=Q131133623|1=Category:Shimazu Island}} in {{ill|lt=Chibu Village|Q1204968|WD=Q1204968|1=Chibu, Shimane}}, formerly called "Watari-jima"'{{ill|lt=Onshū Shichō Gōki|Q11659057|WD=Q11659057|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki}}', vol.4, Dōzen notes on Chibu harbor and mountains., has Watari Shrine at its {{ウィキ座標|36|00|02.9|N|133|02|56.1|E|scale:25000|western end}}, and that this is likely "Watasu Myōjin" and thus the Engishiki Kai Shrine. === Rituals === === Religious rites === ;Annual festival (July 21) :The 1869 'Kyūsha Torishirabechō' states a "grand festival" was held every other year from June 20 to 21. This refers to the boat procession festival. After adoption of the {{ill|lt=Gregorian calendar|Q210953|WD=Q210953|1=Solar calendar}}, the annual festival has been held each year on July 21, and in alternating years the boat procession is held across July 21-22. In years with boat procession, {{ill|lt=Oki Dōzen Kagura|Q11659047|WD=Q11659047|1=Oki-Dozen Kagura}} is dedicated on the night of the 21st. The origin of this boat rite is unknown, but records say a practice that had lapsed in the early modern period was revived in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317|1=Kōka}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1847|Q7644|WD=Q7644|1=1847}}). ;Omushi Festival (October 21) :New rice totaling 1 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|WD=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} 2 {{ill|lt=shō|Q995076|WD=Q995076|1=Sheng (volume)}} is cooked, divided into 12 trays, and offered before the deity. It was formerly held as a minor festival on September 29 ('Kyūsha Torishirabechō'), but after the Gregorian calendar it has been conducted on the current date as the autumn grand festival. === Shrine priests === The Uno family served by hereditary succession. Their family surname was Fujiwara. Tradition says they came to Oki in the late 16th century while fleeing {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, and served from the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun era|1=Kanbun (era)}} (late 17th century) onward ('Kyūsha Torishirabechō'), but left office in early Meiji. === Shrine buildings === The honden is a 1-ken-by-2-ken {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|WD=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper-plate roofing|1=UNKNOWN}}, rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Keiō|Q776775|WD=Q776775|1=Keiō}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1867|Q7715|WD=Q7715|1=1867}}). It connects through a {{ill|lt=heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}. Both heiden and haiden are copper-plate roofed. There is also a mikoshi storehouse. === Precinct shrines === Ise Shrine, Inari Shrine === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.21 San'indō 4, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1983 === External links === *[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/folder/tyosi.html#西ノ島の神社（松浦康麿） Shrines of Nishinoshima (Matsūra Yasumaro)] (Yakihi Shrine) (accessed September 12, 2009) === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043|WD=Q11659043|1=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province}} {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kai Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kai Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Nishinoshima]] [[Category:西ノ島町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Shimane Prefecture]]

Omushi-sai (autumn grand festival) (October 21) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Kai Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Kai Shrine''' (かいじんじゃNishinoshima Tourism Association, "[http://nkk-oki.com/japan/download/ Useful information] Town-walk map, Beppu Port / Mitajiri.") is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Beppu, {{ill|lt=Nishinoshima|Q543193|WD=Q543193|1=Nishinoshima, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|WD=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It stands east of {{ill|lt=Beppu Port (Shimane)|Q11396732|WD=Q11396732|lt=Beppu Port|1=Beppu Port}} and north of the remains of {{ill|lt=Kuroki Gosho|Q11678318|WD=Q11678318|1=kuroki no gosho}}, and is a former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|lt=village shrine|1=Son-sha}} identified with "Kai Shrine, two seats" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' (a {{ill|lt=candidate Shikinaisha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|lt=Shikinaisha candidate|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}). === Enshrined deity === Unknown. The first literary appearance of this shrine is in the 1703 'Dōzen Muramura Shinmei-ki'A record of shrines in {{ill|lt=Dōzen|Q11476216|WD=Q11476216|1=Dōzen}} and their deities., where it is listed as "Rokusha Daimyōjin of Beppu Village" and identified with the Engishiki Kai Shrine, enshrining "one seat of Shiga three deities and one seat of Sumiyoshi three deities." This assigns the two seats in the Engishiki entry to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Watatsumi Sanjin|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}, for six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q4817|WD=Q4817|1=Column}} total. The 1869 'Dōzen Kyūsha Torishirabechō' also reports six deity statues about nine sun in height. Another report by this shrine's priest in early Meiji says that until Meiji they chanted the deity as "Watatsumi no kami no yashiro futakura," but since formal identification in Meiji 5 as Engishiki "Kai Shrine," it has been treated as "deity unknown." The same 'Kyūsha Torishirabechō' reports two cord mirrors in addition to six statues, so if this truly is the Engishiki shrine, those two mirrors may have been the original two sacred objects of "Kai Shrine, two seats." It has thus been pointed out that the old phrase "Watatsumi no kami no yashiro futakura" may mean "Sukunabikona deities, two seats," suggesting the original deity may have been {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuna-dōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}.'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.' === Origin and history === There is a {{ill|lt=kofun|Q1141225|WD=Q1141225|1=Kofun}} behind the {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, and {{ill|lt=obsidian|Q53754|WD=Q53754|lt=obsidian|1=Obsidian}} {{ill|lt=stone arrowheads|Q11587274|WD=Q11587274|1=stone arrowhead}} have been excavated from adjacent land, suggesting an ancient shrine site. If it is truly a Shikinaisha, it may have been worshiped by indigenous {{ill|lt=ama|Q1577970|WD=Q1577970|1=Ama (diving)}} seafaring people.[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/folder/tyosi.html#西ノ島の神社（松浦康麿） Matsūra Yasumaro, "Shrines of Nishinoshima"] In the {{ill|lt=kuninai jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}} '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onshū Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}},' "Senior Third Upper Kaihara Myōjin" and "Senior Fourth Upper Unkaihiko Myōjin" appear, so it may correspond to one of theseMatsūra Yasumaro identifies it with the former; {{ill|lt=Suzuka Tsurane|Q6536446|WD=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}} identifies it with the latter in '{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}'., but premodern origins and development remain unknown, and there are different views on Shikinaisha identification (see below). In the early modern period, it is confirmed to have been called '''Rokusha Daimyōjin''', and from the above 'Dōzen Muramura Shinmei-ki' onward it was identified as a Shikinaisha. In October Meiji 5 it was renamed "Kai Shrine" and ranked as village shrine; since then it has been revered as the tutelary shrine of Beppu. === Alternative theory for Shikinaisha identification === In textual variants of the Unshū-family edition of the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'The Unshū-family edition was collated and block-printed across the reigns of two Matsue-domain lords, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Naritsune|Q11530461|WD=Q11530461|1=Matsudaira Naritsune}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Matsudaira Naritaka|1=UNKNOWN}}., the Chibu District entry for "{{ill|lt=Yurahime Shrine|Q11577626|WD=Q11577626|1=Yurahime Shrine}}" has the note "formerly named Watasu deity." Because the '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onshū Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}}' for Chibu District lists "Yurahime Daimyōjin" separately from "Watasu Myōjin," this is thought to be a note mistakenly shifted from the next line "Kai Shrine" (in the Engishiki, Kai Shrine is listed adjacent to Yurahime Shrine). Based on this, '{{ill|lt=Dai Nihon Shi|Q1156003|WD=Q1156003|lt=Dai Nihon Shi Jingishi|1=Dai Nihonshi}}' and {{ill|lt=Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747|WD=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}}'s '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dainobu|Q11588823|WD=Q11588823|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|1=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dodai}}' argue that {{ill|lt=Shimazu-jima|Q131133623|WD=Q131133623|1=Category:Shimazu Island}} in {{ill|lt=Chibu Village|Q1204968|WD=Q1204968|1=Chibu, Shimane}}, formerly called "Watari-jima"'{{ill|lt=Onshū Shichō Gōki|Q11659057|WD=Q11659057|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki}}', vol.4, Dōzen notes on Chibu harbor and mountains., has Watari Shrine at its {{ウィキ座標|36|00|02.9|N|133|02|56.1|E|scale:25000|western end}}, and that this is likely "Watasu Myōjin" and thus the Engishiki Kai Shrine. === Rituals === === Religious rites === ;Annual festival (July 21) :The 1869 'Kyūsha Torishirabechō' states a "grand festival" was held every other year from June 20 to 21. This refers to the boat procession festival. After adoption of the {{ill|lt=Gregorian calendar|Q210953|WD=Q210953|1=Solar calendar}}, the annual festival has been held each year on July 21, and in alternating years the boat procession is held across July 21-22. In years with boat procession, {{ill|lt=Oki Dōzen Kagura|Q11659047|WD=Q11659047|1=Oki-Dozen Kagura}} is dedicated on the night of the 21st. The origin of this boat rite is unknown, but records say a practice that had lapsed in the early modern period was revived in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317|1=Kōka}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1847|Q7644|WD=Q7644|1=1847}}). ;Omushi Festival (October 21) :New rice totaling 1 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|WD=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} 2 {{ill|lt=shō|Q995076|WD=Q995076|1=Sheng (volume)}} is cooked, divided into 12 trays, and offered before the deity. It was formerly held as a minor festival on September 29 ('Kyūsha Torishirabechō'), but after the Gregorian calendar it has been conducted on the current date as the autumn grand festival. === Shrine priests === The Uno family served by hereditary succession. Their family surname was Fujiwara. Tradition says they came to Oki in the late 16th century while fleeing {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, and served from the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun era|1=Kanbun (era)}} (late 17th century) onward ('Kyūsha Torishirabechō'), but left office in early Meiji. === Shrine buildings === The honden is a 1-ken-by-2-ken {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|WD=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper-plate roofing|1=UNKNOWN}}, rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Keiō|Q776775|WD=Q776775|1=Keiō}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1867|Q7715|WD=Q7715|1=1867}}). It connects through a {{ill|lt=heiden|Q3076699|WD=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}. Both heiden and haiden are copper-plate roofed. There is also a mikoshi storehouse. === Precinct shrines === Ise Shrine, Inari Shrine === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.21 San'indō 4, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1983 === External links === *[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/folder/tyosi.html#西ノ島の神社（松浦康麿） Shrines of Nishinoshima (Matsūra Yasumaro)] (Yakihi Shrine) (accessed September 12, 2009) === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043|WD=Q11659043|1=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province}} {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kai Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kai Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nishinoshima]] [[Category:西ノ島町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Shimane Prefecture]]

It was formerly held as a minor festival on September 29 ('Kyūsha Torishirabechō'), but after the Gregorian calendar it has been conducted on the current date as the autumn grand festival. === Shrine priests === The Uno family served by hereditary succession. Their family surname was Fujiwara. Tradition says they came to Oki in the late 16th century while fleeing {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|qid=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, and served from the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun era|1=Kanbun (era)}} (late 17th century) onward ('Kyūsha Torishirabechō'), but left office in early Meiji. === Shrine buildings === The honden is a 1-ken-by-2-ken {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|qid=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper-plate roofing|1=UNKNOWN}}, rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Keiō|Q776775|qid=Q776775|1=Keiō}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1867|Q7715|qid=Q7715|1=1867}}). It connects through a {{ill|lt=heiden|Q3076699|qid=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|qid=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}. Both heiden and haiden are copper-plate roofed. There is also a mikoshi storehouse. === Precinct shrines === Ise Shrine, Inari Shrine === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.21 San'indō 4, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1983 === External links === *[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/folder/tyosi.html#西ノ島の神社（松浦康麿） Shrines of Nishinoshima (Matsūra Yasumaro)] (Yakihi Shrine) (accessed September 12, 2009) === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043|qid=Q11659043|1=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province}} {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kai Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kai Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nishinoshima]] [[Category:西ノ島町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Shimane Prefecture]]

University Shrine database}} == modern shrine ranking (P13723) == * {{ill|Q134917284|ltq=Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} ** End time: {'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 'ja'} [[JA:海神社 (西ノ島町)]] {{wikidata link|Q11559097}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Kai Shrine |画像 = [[File:海神社.jpg|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|qid=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|qid=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}{{ill|lt=Nishinoshima|Q543193|qid=Q543193|1=Nishinoshima, Shimane}}409 Beppu |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Unknown |社格 = {{ill|lt=small Shikinaisha|Q134917287|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} / former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|qid=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}} copper-plate roofing |別名 = |例祭 = July 21 |神事 = Shinkōsai (boat procession festival) (every other year, July 21-22)
Omushi-sai (autumn grand festival) (October 21) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Kai Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Kai Shrine''' (かいじんじゃNishinoshima Tourism Association, "[http://nkk-oki.com/japan/download/ Useful information] Town-walk map, Beppu Port / Mitajiri.") is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Beppu, {{ill|lt=Nishinoshima|Q543193|qid=Q543193|1=Nishinoshima, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|qid=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|qid=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It stands east of {{ill|lt=Beppu Port (Shimane)|Q11396732|qid=Q11396732|lt=Beppu Port|1=Beppu Port}} and north of the remains of {{ill|lt=Kuroki Gosho|Q11678318|qid=Q11678318|1=kuroki no gosho}}, and is a former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|lt=village shrine|1=Son-sha}} identified with "Kai Shrine, two seats" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' (a {{ill|lt=candidate Shikinaisha|Q135022904|qid=Q135022904|lt=Shikinaisha candidate|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}). === Enshrined deity === Unknown. The first literary appearance of this shrine is in the 1703 'Dōzen Muramura Shinmei-ki'A record of shrines in {{ill|lt=Dōzen|Q11476216|qid=Q11476216|1=Dōzen}} and their deities., where it is listed as "Rokusha Daimyōjin of Beppu Village" and identified with the Engishiki Kai Shrine, enshrining "one seat of Shiga three deities and one seat of Sumiyoshi three deities." This assigns the two seats in the Engishiki entry to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Watatsumi Sanjin|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|qid=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}, for six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q4817|qid=Q4817|1=Column}} total. The 1869 'Dōzen Kyūsha Torishirabechō' also reports six deity statues about nine sun in height. Another report by this shrine's priest in early Meiji says that until Meiji they chanted the deity as "Watatsumi no kami no yashiro futakura," but since formal identification in Meiji 5 as Engishiki "Kai Shrine," it has been treated as "deity unknown." The same 'Kyūsha Torishirabechō' reports two cord mirrors in addition to six statues, so if this truly is the Engishiki shrine, those two mirrors may have been the original two sacred objects of "Kai Shrine, two seats." It has thus been pointed out that the old phrase "Watatsumi no kami no yashiro futakura" may mean "Sukunabikona deities, two seats," suggesting the original deity may have been {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuna-dōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}.'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.' === Origin and history === There is a {{ill|lt=kofun|Q1141225|qid=Q1141225|1=Kofun}} behind the {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|qid=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, and {{ill|lt=obsidian|Q53754|qid=Q53754|lt=obsidian|1=Obsidian}} {{ill|lt=stone arrowheads|Q11587274|qid=Q11587274|1=stone arrowhead}} have been excavated from adjacent land, suggesting an ancient shrine site. If it is truly a Shikinaisha, it may have been worshiped by indigenous {{ill|lt=ama|Q1577970|qid=Q1577970|1=Ama (diving)}} seafaring people.[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/folder/tyosi.html#西ノ島の神社（松浦康麿） Matsūra Yasumaro, "Shrines of Nishinoshima"] In the {{ill|lt=kuninai jinmyōchō|Q11420490|qid=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}} '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onshū Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}},' "Senior Third Upper Kaihara Myōjin" and "Senior Fourth Upper Unkaihiko Myōjin" appear, so it may correspond to one of theseMatsūra Yasumaro identifies it with the former; {{ill|lt=Suzuka Tsurane|Q6536446|qid=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}} identifies it with the latter in '{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|qid=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}'., but premodern origins and development remain unknown, and there are different views on Shikinaisha identification (see below). In the early modern period, it is confirmed to have been called '''Rokusha Daimyōjin''', and from the above 'Dōzen Muramura Shinmei-ki' onward it was identified as a Shikinaisha. In October Meiji 5 it was renamed "Kai Shrine" and ranked as village shrine; since then it has been revered as the tutelary shrine of Beppu. === Alternative theory for Shikinaisha identification === In textual variants of the Unshū-family edition of the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'The Unshū-family edition was collated and block-printed across the reigns of two Matsue-domain lords, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Naritsune|Q11530461|qid=Q11530461|1=Matsudaira Naritsune}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Matsudaira Naritaka|1=UNKNOWN}}., the Chibu District entry for "{{ill|lt=Yurahime Shrine|Q11577626|qid=Q11577626|1=Yurahime Shrine}}" has the note "formerly named Watasu deity." Because the '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onshū Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}}' for Chibu District lists "Yurahime Daimyōjin" separately from "Watasu Myōjin," this is thought to be a note mistakenly shifted from the next line "Kai Shrine" (in the Engishiki, Kai Shrine is listed adjacent to Yurahime Shrine). Based on this, '{{ill|lt=Dai Nihon Shi|Q1156003|qid=Q1156003|lt=Dai Nihon Shi Jingishi|1=Dai Nihonshi}}' and {{ill|lt=Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747|qid=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}}'s '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dainobu|Q11588823|qid=Q11588823|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|1=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dodai}}' argue that {{ill|lt=Shimazu-jima|Q131133623|qid=Q131133623|1=Category:Shimazu Island}} in {{ill|lt=Chibu Village|Q1204968|qid=Q1204968|1=Chibu, Shimane}}, formerly called "Watari-jima"'{{ill|lt=Onshū Shichō Gōki|Q11659057|qid=Q11659057|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki}}', vol.4, Dōzen notes on Chibu harbor and mountains., has Watari Shrine at its {{ウィキ座標|36|00|02.9|N|133|02|56.1|E|scale:25000|western end}}, and that this is likely "Watasu Myōjin" and thus the Engishiki Kai Shrine. === Rituals === === Religious rites === ;Annual festival (July 21) :The 1869 'Kyūsha Torishirabechō' states a "grand festival" was held every other year from June 20 to 21. This refers to the boat procession festival. After adoption of the {{ill|lt=Gregorian calendar|Q210953|qid=Q210953|1=Solar calendar}}, the annual festival has been held each year on July 21, and in alternating years the boat procession is held across July 21-22. In years with boat procession, {{ill|lt=Oki Dōzen Kagura|Q11659047|qid=Q11659047|1=Oki-Dozen Kagura}} is dedicated on the night of the 21st. The origin of this boat rite is unknown, but records say a practice that had lapsed in the early modern period was revived in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|qid=Q1325317|1=Kōka}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1847|Q7644|qid=Q7644|1=1847}}). ;Omushi Festival (October 21) :New rice totaling 1 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|qid=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} 2 {{ill|lt=shō|Q995076|qid=Q995076|1=Sheng (volume)}} is cooked, divided into 12 trays, and offered before the deity. It was formerly held as a minor festival on September 29 ('Kyūsha Torishirabechō'), but after the Gregorian calendar it has been conducted on the current date as the autumn grand festival. === Shrine priests === The Uno family served by hereditary succession. Their family surname was Fujiwara. Tradition says they came to Oki in the late 16th century while fleeing {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|qid=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, and served from the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun era|1=Kanbun (era)}} (late 17th century) onward ('Kyūsha Torishirabechō'), but left office in early Meiji. === Shrine buildings === The honden is a 1-ken-by-2-ken {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|qid=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper-plate roofing|1=UNKNOWN}}, rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Keiō|Q776775|qid=Q776775|1=Keiō}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1867|Q7715|qid=Q7715|1=1867}}). It connects through a {{ill|lt=heiden|Q3076699|qid=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|qid=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}. Both heiden and haiden are copper-plate roofed. There is also a mikoshi storehouse. === Precinct shrines === Ise Shrine, Inari Shrine === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.21 San'indō 4, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1983 === External links === *[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/folder/tyosi.html#西ノ島の神社（松浦康麿） Shrines of Nishinoshima (Matsūra Yasumaro)] (Yakihi Shrine) (accessed September 12, 2009) === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043|qid=Q11659043|1=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province}} {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kai Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kai Shrine]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nishinoshima]] [[Category:西ノ島町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Shimane Prefecture]]

{{wikidata link|Q11559097}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Kai Shrine |画像 = [[File:海神社.jpg|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|qid=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|qid=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}{{ill|lt=Nishinoshima|Q543193|qid=Q543193|1=Nishinoshima, Shimane}}409 Beppu |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Unknown |社格 = {{ill|lt=small Shikinaisha|Q134917287|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} / former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|qid=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}} copper-plate roofing |別名 = |例祭 = July 21 |神事 = Shinkōsai (boat procession festival) (every other year, July 21-22)
Omushi-sai (autumn grand festival) (October 21) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Kai Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Kai Shrine''' (かいじんじゃNishinoshima Tourism Association, "[http://nkk-oki.com/japan/download/ Useful information] Town-walk map, Beppu Port / Mitajiri.") is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Beppu, {{ill|lt=Nishinoshima|Q543193|qid=Q543193|1=Nishinoshima, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Oki District|Q735735|qid=Q735735|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|qid=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It stands east of {{ill|lt=Beppu Port (Shimane)|Q11396732|qid=Q11396732|lt=Beppu Port|1=Beppu Port}} and north of the remains of {{ill|lt=Kuroki Gosho|Q11678318|qid=Q11678318|1=kuroki no gosho}}, and is a former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|lt=village shrine|1=Son-sha}} identified with "Kai Shrine, two seats" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' (a {{ill|lt=candidate Shikinaisha|Q135022904|qid=Q135022904|lt=Shikinaisha candidate|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}). === Enshrined deity === Unknown. The first literary appearance of this shrine is in the 1703 'Dōzen Muramura Shinmei-ki'A record of shrines in {{ill|lt=Dōzen|Q11476216|qid=Q11476216|1=Dōzen}} and their deities., where it is listed as "Rokusha Daimyōjin of Beppu Village" and identified with the Engishiki Kai Shrine, enshrining "one seat of Shiga three deities and one seat of Sumiyoshi three deities." This assigns the two seats in the Engishiki entry to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Watatsumi Sanjin|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|qid=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}, for six {{ill|lt=pillars|Q4817|qid=Q4817|1=Column}} total. The 1869 'Dōzen Kyūsha Torishirabechō' also reports six deity statues about nine sun in height. Another report by this shrine's priest in early Meiji says that until Meiji they chanted the deity as "Watatsumi no kami no yashiro futakura," but since formal identification in Meiji 5 as Engishiki "Kai Shrine," it has been treated as "deity unknown." The same 'Kyūsha Torishirabechō' reports two cord mirrors in addition to six statues, so if this truly is the Engishiki shrine, those two mirrors may have been the original two sacred objects of "Kai Shrine, two seats." It has thus been pointed out that the old phrase "Watatsumi no kami no yashiro futakura" may mean "Sukunabikona deities, two seats," suggesting the original deity may have been {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukuna-dōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}.'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.' === Origin and history === There is a {{ill|lt=kofun|Q1141225|qid=Q1141225|1=Kofun}} behind the {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|qid=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, and {{ill|lt=obsidian|Q53754|qid=Q53754|lt=obsidian|1=Obsidian}} {{ill|lt=stone arrowheads|Q11587274|qid=Q11587274|1=stone arrowhead}} have been excavated from adjacent land, suggesting an ancient shrine site. If it is truly a Shikinaisha, it may have been worshiped by indigenous {{ill|lt=ama|Q1577970|qid=Q1577970|1=Ama (diving)}} seafaring people.[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/folder/tyosi.html#西ノ島の神社（松浦康麿） Matsūra Yasumaro, "Shrines of Nishinoshima"] In the {{ill|lt=kuninai jinmyōchō|Q11420490|qid=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}} '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onshū Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}},' "Senior Third Upper Kaihara Myōjin" and "Senior Fourth Upper Unkaihiko Myōjin" appear, so it may correspond to one of theseMatsūra Yasumaro identifies it with the former; {{ill|lt=Suzuka Tsurane|Q6536446|qid=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}} identifies it with the latter in '{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|qid=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}'., but premodern origins and development remain unknown, and there are different views on Shikinaisha identification (see below). In the early modern period, it is confirmed to have been called '''Rokusha Daimyōjin''', and from the above 'Dōzen Muramura Shinmei-ki' onward it was identified as a Shikinaisha. In October Meiji 5 it was renamed "Kai Shrine" and ranked as village shrine; since then it has been revered as the tutelary shrine of Beppu. === Alternative theory for Shikinaisha identification === In textual variants of the Unshū-family edition of the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'The Unshū-family edition was collated and block-printed across the reigns of two Matsue-domain lords, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Naritsune|Q11530461|qid=Q11530461|1=Matsudaira Naritsune}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Matsudaira Naritaka|1=UNKNOWN}}., the Chibu District entry for "{{ill|lt=Yurahime Shrine|Q11577626|qid=Q11577626|1=Yurahime Shrine}}" has the note "formerly named Watasu deity." Because the '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Onshū Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}}' for Chibu District lists "Yurahime Daimyōjin" separately from "Watasu Myōjin," this is thought to be a note mistakenly shifted from the next line "Kai Shrine" (in the Engishiki, Kai Shrine is listed adjacent to Yurahime Shrine). Based on this, '{{ill|lt=Dai Nihon Shi|Q1156003|qid=Q1156003|lt=Dai Nihon Shi Jingishi|1=Dai Nihonshi}}' and {{ill|lt=Ban Nobutomo|Q4346747|qid=Q4346747|1=Ban Nobutomo}}'s '{{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dainobu|Q11588823|qid=Q11588823|lt=Jinmyōchō Kōshō|1=Jinmyōchō Kōshō Dodai}}' argue that {{ill|lt=Shimazu-jima|Q131133623|qid=Q131133623|1=Category:Shimazu Island}} in {{ill|lt=Chibu Village|Q1204968|qid=Q1204968|1=Chibu, Shimane}}, formerly called "Watari-jima"'{{ill|lt=Onshū Shichō Gōki|Q11659057|qid=Q11659057|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki}}', vol.4, Dōzen notes on Chibu harbor and mountains., has Watari Shrine at its {{ウィキ座標|36|00|02.9|N|133|02|56.1|E|scale:25000|western end}}, and that this is likely "Watasu Myōjin" and thus the Engishiki Kai Shrine. === Rituals === === Religious rites === ;Annual festival (July 21) :The 1869 'Kyūsha Torishirabechō' states a "grand festival" was held every other year from June 20 to 21. This refers to the boat procession festival. After adoption of the {{ill|lt=Gregorian calendar|Q210953|qid=Q210953|1=Solar calendar}}, the annual festival has been held each year on July 21, and in alternating years the boat procession is held across July 21-22. In years with boat procession, {{ill|lt=Oki Dōzen Kagura|Q11659047|qid=Q11659047|1=Oki-Dozen Kagura}} is dedicated on the night of the 21st. The origin of this boat rite is unknown, but records say a practice that had lapsed in the early modern period was revived in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|qid=Q1325317|1=Kōka}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1847|Q7644|qid=Q7644|1=1847}}). ;Omushi Festival (October 21) :New rice totaling 1 {{ill|lt=to|Q1078168|qid=Q1078168|1=To (unit)}} 2 {{ill|lt=shō|Q995076|qid=Q995076|1=Sheng (volume)}} is cooked, divided into 12 trays, and offered before the deity. It was formerly held as a minor festival on September 29 ('Kyūsha Torishirabechō'), but after the Gregorian calendar it has been conducted on the current date as the autumn grand festival. === Shrine priests === The Uno family served by hereditary succession. Their family surname was Fujiwara. Tradition says they came to Oki in the late 16th century while fleeing {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|qid=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, and served from the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|lt=Kanbun era|1=Kanbun (era)}} (late 17th century) onward ('Kyūsha Torishirabechō'), but left office in early Meiji. === Shrine buildings === The honden is a 1-ken-by-2-ken {{ill|lt=Oki-zukuri|Q11659055|qid=Q11659055|1=Oki-zukuri}} structure with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=copper-plate roofing|1=UNKNOWN}}, rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Keiō|Q776775|qid=Q776775|1=Keiō}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1867|Q7715|qid=Q7715|1=1867}}). It connects through a {{ill|lt=heiden|Q3076699|qid=Q3076699|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} to the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|qid=Q3125745|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}. Both heiden and haiden are copper-plate roofed. There is also a mikoshi storehouse. === Precinct shrines === Ise Shrine, Inari Shrine === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai (ed.), 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku,' vol.21 San'indō 4, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōgakkan University Press|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1983 === External links === *[http://www.lares.dti.ne.jp/~takuhi/folder/tyosi.html#西ノ島の神社（松浦康麿） Shrines of Nishinoshima (Matsūra Yasumaro)] (Yakihi Shrine) (accessed September 12, 2009) === Related item === * {{ill|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province|Q11659043|qid=Q11659043|1=List of Shikinaisha in Oki Province}} {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kai Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kai Shrine]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nishinoshima]] [[Category:西ノ島町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Shimane Prefecture]]
